---
title: "FME support for MCP"
author:
  - name: Ralph Straumann
    url: https://ralphstraumann.ch
date: "2026-07-09 18:46"
image: "mcp-architecture.png"
description: 'Safe Software has turned #FME into an #MCP bridge (to the #Esri #ArcGIS platform, for example): FME workspaces can now be published as AI-ready tools and served to any MCP client via FME Flow. Safe Software frames this as turning GIS platforms into something #AI agents can operate conversationally, with notably pitching FME as a "data guardian" shielding raw data from the AI clients that call it.'
---

MCP[^mcp] capabilities are now available "bi-directionally" in FME[^fme]. The
[`MCPCaller` Transformer][caller][^caller] is available from FME 2026.1
onwards, and FME Flow[^fme-flow] can act as an MCP Server since FME 2026.2.

The latter scenario is described in detail in a recent [article by Safe 
Software][article]. Basically, the new MCP capability means you can author a 
data workflow in FME Form[^fme-form] and register it as an AI- or agent-ready 
tool with an MCP server run by FME Flow. Once published and registered, the tool 
(your workspace) can be called from any MCP client. 

![MCP architecture based on FME Flow's MCP Server](mcp-in-fme.png "MCP 
architecture based on FME Flow's MCP Server")

The [article][article] gives the following account of the workflow:  

> - **Prepare and publish**: Replace the workspace’s terminal step (...) with 
an MCP writer, which defines what comes back to the client: text, structured 
content (JSON[^json]), images, resources, or embedded content. Adding it flags the 
workspace as an MCP tool. (...) Return only what the client needs (...). Keep 
input parameter types simple (text, numbers, booleans, dates, dropdowns) (...).
> <br><br>
> - **Register the tool**: A single [FME] Flow instance can host multiple MCP 
servers, each with multiple tools, organized by domain or user group. (...) Add 
a tool pointing at your published workspace, then describe each parameter (...). 
The pattern throughout is: define the field, give the constraints, and say when 
the client needs more information.
> <br><br>
> - **Connect a client and test it out**: Most AI clients let you add an MCP 
server by URL. A deliberately messy prompt is a good stress test (...) 
Underneath, [the task] is still a standard [FME] Flow job, visible in job 
history with its inputs and processing details; the only difference is that MCP 
triggered it.

In the [article][article], Safe Software goes into more detail about the 
workflow and lists some caveats and principles for designing good MCP tools. 
It also describes a more advanced scenario involving an AI planner-in-a-loop 
that can invoke multiple tools in a single workflow and combine their results.

While Safe Software underlines that the principles shown in the article apply to any integration, not just ArcGIS-based ones, I found the statement that this turns 
ArcGIS into a recipient for conversational agents a particularly interesting 
notion: "(...) a small set of focused tools (...) turns ArcGIS into something an 
agent can operate conversationally." Meanwhile, Esri seems to be working on 
similar ideas and has published an [Early Adopter site][early] on MCP in GIS 
which states: 

> Esri is working towards exposing GIS capabilities via Model Context Protocol 
(MCP) across ArcGIS Enterprise, Online and Location Platform[^beta]. Our intent 
with this work is to help you access GIS data and functionality through AI 
applications. 

Finally, the [Safe Software article][article] extols information security as a 
key positive aspect^[Note the capitalisation in the quote.] of the presented 
usage scenario: 

> You can also control exactly what data is exposed to AI in any deployment 
using FME as a Data Guardian. The MCP clients never get direct access to your 
raw data, and in fact have no idea what the raw data looks like.

If MCP within the FME ecosystem interests you, Safe Software has much more 
MCP-related content on a [dedicated "Solutions" page][page] and offers (past 
and upcoming) [webinars][webinars] on the topic.

[^mcp]: Model Context Protocol, an open standard (introduced by Anthropic in
late 2024) that lets AI applications connect to external tools, data sources,
and services through a common interface.
[^fme]: Safe Software's Feature Manipulation Engine.
[^caller]: The `MCPCaller` serves as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) client that
can connect to an MCP server to call tools to work with resources and prompts.
[^fme-flow]: FME Flow is Safe Software's server product for automating,
scheduling, and hosting FME workspaces, formerly known as FME Server.
[^fme-form]: FME Form is Safe Software's desktop application for authoring FME
workspaces, formerly known as FME Desktop or FME Workbench.
[^json]: JavaScript Object Notation.
[^beta]: MCP support for the [ArcGIS Location
Platform](https://www.esri.com/arcgis-blog/products/platform/developers/mcp-support-beta-and-arcgis-static-maps-service-in-arcgis-location-platform-release)
is currently in beta.


[caller]: https://docs.safe.com/fme/html/FME-Form-Documentation/FME-Transformers/Transformers/pkg-mcpcaller/mcpcaller-pkg.htm
[article]: https://fme.safe.com/blog/2026/07/turning-fme-workspaces-into-mcp-tools-connecting-arcgis-to-ai/
[early]: https://earlyadopter.esri.com/key/MCPsupportforArcGISLocationServices
[page]: https://fme.safe.com/platform/solutions/mcp/
[webinars]: https://fme.safe.com/webinars/
